speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 4, 1996. The survivor is Lili Meier, and this is tape three. So an act of kindness and an aspirin saved your life? An act of kindness by a German--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --guard? And you arrived at Stuttgart. And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About five weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like in Stuttgart?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were a little bit better.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made them better?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- they just had better living conditions. And they were also dressed better, the people, than we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get new clothing while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stuttgart?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In a munition factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food like in Stuttgart?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you treated well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fairly well.

speaker: interviewer
text: The sanitary conditions-- what were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they-- they were much better, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were able to clean yourself and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sleep in bunks also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were like-- yes, bunks. Yes, they were bunks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many women to a bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also about 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Equivalent to like they were in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many women were in a barrack, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember offhand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your aunts with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your aunts.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just-- they just decided that there's gonna be a [GERMAN], and whoever they gonna pick, they'll go. And I was looking for my aunts, but I didn't see them. I have one living aunt. She's 86 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in Birkenau or in Stuttgart, did you receive any word of what was happening in the war or what was happening outside of the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we were still in-- in Birkenau when we heard that there was an attempt of-- somebody want to kill Hitler but didn't succeed, unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you left Stuttgart? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing as before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go when you left Stuttgart?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were put on a train with a German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] SS woman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took us on a train again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was it now?

speaker: interviewee
text: January, February, March. March 15, they took us on a train again. And she was there also. Still with the aspirins, just in case.

speaker: interviewer
text: That same nice guard.

speaker: interviewee
text: That same woman, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they took you on the train, did they give you any food or water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Water, they gave us. They gave us some halfway decent food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this also a cattle car? Cattle train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were also crowded into--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all women, or were there men on the train too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. All woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about day and-- day and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people die on this train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see any dead people.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the train stopped and the doors opened, what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just were told to get out, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do-- were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where. When you got off the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- we were taken to Nordhausen-Dora. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they laid us-- the three sick girls with the typhus, with the-- with the fever, and they put us on a-- on-- in a corner of a theater. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And all of a sudden, I heard men talking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one said to the other in Czechoslovakian, I think this one, we will-- we'll be able to save. But not the other two. The other twos are-- are-- are too far gone. And sure, they took us to a so-called hospital in-- in Dora there, in Nordhausen-Dora. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- he was a doctor before, you know. He was also from Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Jewish, this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he said, what is your name? And I told him. And he said, where do you come from? I told him from Bilky. He says, I had a very nice lady. She was my patient. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was an obstetrician. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, her name was-- my mother's name was Esther Jacob. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Did you know her? I says, yeah. She-- she was my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He says, how many of you are here? I said, I'm the only one from that family left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was very, very nice to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was an incident in Dora, day after we arrived in-- in Dora, there was-- they decided to take all the Jews-- it was towards the end of the war. And they took all the men away, and they sent them to Bergen-Belsen. I didn't know until I was in Germany with Eric, and people would take us for-- to see the sceneries. And once-- this was a couple years ago-- we were there in Hildesheim. And he stopped the car on a hill. And I looked down, and I said to him-- I says, these look like the-- the Harz Mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this man says to me, how would you know the Harz Mountains? I says, I lived there. What do you mean, you lived there? I says, I lived there. I says, I-- you know Dora, Nordhausen-Dora? He says, yeah, I know. I says, that's where I lived. He says, when? I says, when I was liberated in '45. [LAUGHS] So you arrived in Dora in March of 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were ill, and they put you in the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the hospital like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was all right. Because those two prisoners who found-- found out there on the floor in the theater-- on-- on the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and they were constantly knocking on the window. We have some food for you. We have [INAUDIBLE]. And that's-- that's how I survived. And there was also a Czechoslovakian doctor in Dora who went up in the crematorium chimney, made sure that there is no-- there's not gonna be any people burning. And he sent out to-- to the Allies, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, the-- where they are and-- and where they should go and everything. And he was supposed to be hanged. There was already-- they were talking about-- because we all had to watch when somebody was electrocuted or something. We had to watch that. But it turned out that when they came in, the Americans, on April 11, and right away they asked for him. And they got together. It is now what can we do for you. You helped us so much. He says, you can do only one thing for me. Give me a bus from-- from Prague here. I'm gonna take my-- all-- all my people back there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. John Cespiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Dora for a month, approximately, before liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the hospital that whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever on a death march?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] During the time you were in the hospital, then you received food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were the-- the prisoners there, the Czechoslovakian, would constantly come and bring me. And I says, don't bring it. I can't eat all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Jewish prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Czechoslovakian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So again you received an act of kindness--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --which kept you alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Now can I go to something? I'm not trying to rush you. If I do, please tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I just wanted you to tell me about the day that you were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was laying in the hospital. And all of a sudden, we heard noises. The hospital was on a hill. And across about 400, 500 feet-- excuse me-- was the crematorium. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they came in, the prisoners. And they said-- get yourself together, he says, because you hear the-- the noise down in the valley? The Americans just marched in, and we are liberated. And was a-- it was a good day for us to know. And all I remember is going out from the hospital, down, and I came about halfway, and I just passed out. But the other ones were still behind, and they picked me up. And they carried me in-- in a German barrack and laid me on a bed. And it was April, and just having on that little thin dress with the lice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, you don't have to worry now anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We are liberated. I says, yeah, where are our parents? Right away that was the first thing. But [SIGHS] I don't know. Somehow God gives us strength. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So this was a couple years ago. We were in Germany. And another thing happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We met in the Jewish cemetery where Eric's father-- not Eric's father, but the ashes of him, they buried there in the cemetery, his mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were there visiting the grave. And he-- he remembered some neighbors where he grew up. And he-- I looked back in-- in the cemetery. We were already on the outside. And there was a man with students cleaning the [YIDDISH], the headstones. And I looked back, and I said, Eric, gee, I says. This must be a special person. He doesn't look Jewish. And this is a bunch of students are cleaning and washing the headstones. And then he came out, introduced himself to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were talking. But I didn't-- he didn't know Eric, or Eric didn't know him. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And we became good friends. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We call one another. He calls us. We call-- call him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to him, the last time I was there, I says, I need a big favor of you. He says, you don't have to beg for it. Just tell me what you want. I'll do anything for you. You know that. I said, how could I find out where I am, how far I am? You know, so I would like to find out, if you could find out, is [SIGHS] about liberation. He says, you don't remember? Oh, I says. I remember. It was April 11. He says, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I have a friend living in Nordhausen. She's the only one who could help us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I say, I want to find that lady, that-- that German guard. I say, I do anything. I'll bring her to America. I says, she saved my life. He said, well, I have somebody who would be able to help us. And sure enough, she helped us. My name was in that transport arriving in Dora-- everything. And I was invited last-- it-- it-- was it two years ago, for 50th-- 50th year of liberation. I was invited to go. Eric can't fly, so I took my Esther with me. And we met her there. And I want to thank you very much, I says, but I'll thank you again a million times. Find me that lady. I say, I want to reciprocate for saving my life. But as of now, I talk to her. I write to her. And she always says, you know, Lili, as soon as I find [? up-- ?] find out something, I'll let you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were liberated--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: --on April 11.

speaker: interviewee
text: On April 11, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1945. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the other prisoners picked you up and took you to a German barrack and laid you down on a bed because you were so weak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they just tried their best, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Americans. And no sooner were-- were we liberated, I was taken again to the hospital there to-- to lay in the-- on a bed. And a German soldier came over. He says, you're so sick. Where's the doctor? I says, I don't know. I don't know. But I-- I couldn't speak to him in English because I didn't speak English.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean English-- an English soldier came to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. An American soldier. So going back to Nordhausen-Dora, like I said, we were invited to go to the anniversary, to celebrate the 50th anniversary. And this is-- this other American soldier is standing there. This was now, last April.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, miss, do you speak English? I said, a little bit. I says, you know, 50 years ago today we liberated this camp. And I didn't know where to go. I ran to the crematorium. I never saw anything like it. I ran to all-- all over, to the German barracks, and all I hear is noises. I-- so [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they took me back to the hospital. But going back to April 11 of this-- this is '96, '95, right? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So he said to me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 50 years ago today we liberated this camp. And there was-- I don't know where to run, to crematorium, to the hospital, or where. I come to the hospital, and I see this young lady screaming, hysterical, burning up with fever. Would you by any chance know what happened to that girl? I looked at him. I start crying. I says, sir. I am that little girl. And in my honor, they opened up the theater for the city in Nordhausen. What else happened on the day of liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was in love.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Would you happen to know if they used the crematoriums right up until the time of liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three weeks after liberation. And he got the bus to go back to Prague and come back for his people. And unfortunately, he-- he got cancer, and he passed away. But I used to go to Prague a-- a-- a lot. And God forbid if I came to Prague because of the papers coming, being able to come to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And God forbid if I came to Prague and he found out that-- he would call my-- the lawyer, because I would go to the lawyer to help us get the papers. He-- he would call him. Was she in town? Was she in-- in the city? And I told him, tell him the truth. Yes or no. He was a good friend. So you remained in Nordhausen for another three weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then I--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: During that time? Not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: You began to feel better?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I came back to the living.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then there was another prisoner, but-- because with the Czechoslovakians, we got along. And he came over and he says, you know, you come with me. I'm taking you home. Because I already talked to my wife on the telephone. And we gonna nurse you back till you get real healthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, but what will your wife think? He says, don't worry what she'll think. You'll see what kind of wife I had. He was, you know, a-- a-- always against the government, communism. They arrested him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used to go to Auschwitz and bring me-- send me always pictures from there, you know, in-- in Auschwitz. There's-- they are from each country like Hebrew and Hungarian, Czechoslovakian written. You were there, so I don't have to tell you. But it was, [? I think, really ?] in Auschwitz, the-- I went back the first time. Is-- it was like a bad dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: But where did-- after Nordhausen, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: To where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in Moravia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, we were-- we were able to get on trains without paying. And I-- they-- they told us that whatever you see, whatever you want, you just go ahead and get it, and-- and-- and take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- about two days. Because we came to Prague, and from Prague it wasn't that far already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like there for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in very late at night. But we-- he notified his wife that we are on our way. And some friends were-- of his were able to take us by car to his house, to their house. And I [? never ?] forget. I got up in the morning and went to have coffee. And all of a sudden, I-- I see a man. And he was dressed like a policeman. I started screaming and carrying on like a lunatic. They didn't know what happened to me. But finally, you know, they appeased me. 'Cause he says, look, this is [? Mary's ?] brother. He didn't come to hurt you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and I have the same thing in Miami when we arrived in Miami. We were walking on [INAUDIBLE] Street there, and all of a sudden I see policeman. Minute I saw policeman, I was screaming, I-- you know? How long did you remain with this gentleman and his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: For about five weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baum. Baum. Baum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we went into-- in-- in the city there in Moravia. And all of a-- and the trains were flocking to Europe with the survivors. And all of a sudden, I see a friend on the train. She hollers, what are you doing here? I said, nothing. I'm gonna go home soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- and she says, where is home? I says, well, I'm going home. I-- I'll never forgive myself. I could have gone right away, because the same day I found that album, some American-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] well, he was a soldier. But he had a very high rank. And he said to me-- he start talking to me in English. And I says, I'm sorry. I don't speak English. Well, what do you speak? So I told him. So he brought over some people who were able to translate the language.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, you know, you have something of very great importance. We need this. Meant the album. I-- he says, we would like very much if you would give it to us. We gonna have trials here, and we'll need that proof. I says, I'm not gonna give it. If I can't go there and hold on to the album, not giving it away.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned an album. Where did you find this album?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found it in a vacant German barrack on the day of liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the album?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was in a nightstand next to where the-- my fellow prisoners left me. And then when they came to pick me up, I says, look, I found an Auschwitz album, which was six months before-- later and 500 miles away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in this album?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pictures of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of the people that I grew up with, that we were friends or we were relatives. He says, you wouldn't be able to take it because they'll arrest you, maybe, somewheres on the border, and they'll take it away from you. As it turn out, they did take it away from-- from me, the Russians. But they gave it back 'cause I carried on like a lunatic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many, many pictures in this album?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were like 200 pictures pasted in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you found this album, were you aware that these pictures had been taken in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of how important this album was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was very ignorant about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you took this album, and you kept it with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I kept it with me. When my daughter was-- our daughter was born, Esther, that album was under her mattress in the carriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a tape break now.
